BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1 # cargo-oxide 2 3 Cargo subcommand for building and running Rust GPU programs with cuda-oxide. 4 5 Replaces the previous `xtask` pattern with a proper cargo subcommand that works both inside the cuda-oxide repo (for developers) and externally (for users who `cargo install`). 6 7 ## Installation 8 9 **Internal developers** (inside the cuda-oxide repo): no installation needed. The workspace alias makes `cargo oxide` work immediately. 10 11 **External users**: 12 13 Install with the project's pinned nightly toolchain: 14 15 ```bash 16 cargo +nightly-2026-04-03 install --git https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide.git cargo-oxide 17 ``` 18 19 On first run, `cargo-oxide` will automatically fetch and build the codegen backend if it's not already available. 20 21 ## Usage 22 23 ```bash 24 cargo oxide new my_project # scaffold a new cuda-oxide project 25 cargo oxide new my_project --async # scaffold with async template (tokio + cuda-async) 26 cargo oxide run vecadd # build + run an example 27 cargo oxide build vecadd # compile only (no run) 28 cargo oxide emit-ltoir vecadd --arch sm_100 # device code -> .ltoir (Tile/SIMT interop) 29 cargo oxide build -- -p my_app # arbitrary cargo build through cuda-oxide 30 cargo oxide test # cargo test with cuda-oxide defaults 31 cargo oxide test -- -p my_app # arbitrary cargo test through cuda-oxide 32 cargo oxide pipeline vecadd # verbose pipeline dump 33 # (MIR -> dialect-mir -> LLVM dialect -> LLVM IR -> PTX) 34 cargo oxide sanitize vecadd # build + run under NVIDIA Compute Sanitizer 35 cargo oxide debug vecadd --tui # build + launch cuda-gdb 36 cargo oxide fmt # format all crates 37 cargo oxide fmt --check # check formatting 38 cargo oxide doctor # validate environment 39 cargo oxide setup # explicitly build the codegen backend 40 ``` 41 42 ### Flags 43 44 | Flag | Applies to | Description | 45 |------------------------------|----------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------| 46 | `--emit-nvvm-ir` | run, build, pipeline | Generate NVVM IR for libNVVM | 47 | `--arch ` | run, sanitize, build, test, pipeline, emit-ltoir | Target architecture override | 48 | `--features ` | run, sanitize, debug, build, build passthrough, emit-ltoir | Comma-separated cargo features to enable | 49 | `--bin ` | run, sanitize, debug | Specific binary target to build/run | 50 | `--tool ` | sanitize | Compute Sanitizer tool: `memcheck`, `racecheck`, `initcheck`, or `synccheck` | 51 | `-o, --output

` | emit-ltoir | Output path for the `.ltoir` artifact | 52 | `--cargo-target-dir ` | build/test passthrough | Cargo target directory | 53 | `--device-codegen-crate ` | build/test passthrough | Comma-separated device owner crate filter | 54 | `--device-cfg ` | build/test passthrough | Append `--cfg NAME` to rustflags | 55 | `-v, --verbose` | run, sanitize, build, test, emit-ltoir | Show detailed compilation output | 56 | `--no-fmad` | run, sanitize, build, emit-ltoir, pipeline | Keep ordinary multiply and add/subtract operations separate | 57 | `--async` | new | Use the async template | 58 | `--cgdb` | debug | Use cgdb instead of cuda-gdb | 59 | `--tui` | debug | Use GDB's TUI interface | 60 | `--check` | fmt | Check formatting only | 61 62 `--arch` is required for `emit-ltoir` and explicit NVVM IR output because those 63 artifacts are architecture-specific. Without an override, `run` detects the 64 local GPU, while `build` and `pipeline` use the compiler's feature-based target 65 so they remain useful for cross-compilation. 66 67 `--no-fmad` (or `CUDA_OXIDE_NO_FMA=1`) disables implicit contraction of an 68 ordinary multiply followed by an add or subtract, so the two operations round 69 separately. Explicit fused operations such as `f32::mul_add` remain fused. 70 For NVVM IR and LTOIR, cuda-oxide records the policy in matching `.options` 71 and versioned `.target` files and passes `-fma=0` through libNVVM and 72 nvJitLink. Keep both sidecars with the artifact when another build system 73 consumes it. 74 75 ## Commands 76 77 ### `cargo oxide run ` 78 79 Builds the codegen backend, compiles the example with the custom backend, and runs it. This is the primary command for day-to-day development. 80 81 When neither `--arch` nor `CUDA_OXIDE_TARGET` is set, `run` detects the 82 compute capability of CUDA device 0 and targets that architecture so the 83 generated PTX can load on the local GPU. Use `--arch ` or 84 `CUDA_OXIDE_TARGET=` to override this for a specific device or 85 cross-target workflow. 86 87 ```bash 88 cargo oxide run vecadd 89 cargo oxide run gemm_sol 90 cargo oxide run device_ffi_test --emit-nvvm-ir --arch sm_120 91 cargo oxide run cutile_inter_kernel 92 ``` 93 94 Interop examples can declare extra cuda-oxide device crates with 95 `[[package.metadata.cuda-oxide.device-crates]]`, plus optional 96 `[package.metadata.cuda-oxide.interop]` metadata. `cargo oxide run` builds those 97 device crates with `rustc-codegen-cuda`, writes their PTX to the 98 configured location, and then builds/runs the host crate normally. 99 `cutile_inter_kernel` uses this path: 100 the host crate is a cutile-rs program, while `simt/` is a cuda-oxide SIMT PTX 101 crate loaded by the host at runtime. 102 103 ### `cargo oxide sanitize ` 104 105 Builds an example like `cargo oxide run`, then executes the produced release 106 binary under NVIDIA Compute Sanitizer. `memcheck` is the default tool; use 107 `--tool racecheck`, `--tool initcheck`, or `--tool synccheck` for shared-memory 108 hazard, uninitialized global-memory, or synchronization checks. 109 110 ```bash 111 cargo oxide sanitize vecadd --arch sm_75 112 cargo oxide sanitize sharedmem --tool racecheck 113 cargo oxide sanitize debug --tool synccheck -- --kernel-name kns=sync 114 cargo oxide sanitize vecadd -- --leak-check full 115 cargo oxide sanitize my_app -- --leak-check full -- --app-flag value 116 ``` 117 118 Arguments after the first `--` are passed to `compute-sanitizer` before the 119 executable. A second `--` splits target program arguments, matching 120 `compute-sanitizer [options] [your-program] [your-program-options]`. 121 122 `sanitize` uses Cargo's reported executable artifact, so custom binary names, 123 workspace layouts, configured target directories, and host target triples do 124 not require path guessing. Device line tables are enabled by default while 125 normal optimization remains on. 126 127 Compute Sanitizer's own error exit code defaults to zero. This command supplies 128 `--error-exitcode 86` unless you pass an explicit value, so tool findings fail 129 scripts and CI. If you intentionally pass `--error-exitcode 0`, inspect the 130 printed report; a zero process status no longer implies that the report was 131 clean. Options such as `--check-exit-code no` and `--require-cuda-init no` also 132 weaken what a zero status proves. The wrapper reports completion and reminds 133 you to inspect the sanitizer output instead of declaring the report clean from 134 status alone. 135 NVIDIA recommends treating the tools as complementary: `racecheck`, 136 `initcheck`, and `synccheck` do not replace `memcheck` memory-access checking. 137 138 ### `cargo oxide build ` 139 140 Same as `run` but stops after compilation. Useful for examples that require hardware you don't have (e.g., Blackwell tensor cores). 141 142 ```bash 143 cargo oxide build htens # compiles PTX, doesn't try to run on GPU 144 cargo oxide build tcgen05 # sm_100a only, but PTX generation works anywhere 145 ``` 146 147 `build` also has a passthrough mode for normal Cargo workspaces. Put the Cargo 148 arguments after `--`; cargo-oxide supplies the backend, target architecture, 149 configured environment, and optional device owner filters. 150 151 ```bash 152 cargo oxide build -- # plain `cargo build` 153 cargo oxide build --arch sm_86 -- -p my_app --bin app --release 154 cargo oxide build --cargo-target-dir target/cuda -- -p my_app --release 155 cargo oxide build --device-codegen-crate gpu-kernels,math_gpu -- -p my_app 156 ``` 157 158 The owner filter is matched against rustc crate names, so Cargo package hyphens 159 are normalized to underscores (`gpu-kernels` matches `gpu_kernels`). The CUDA 160 backend still compiles host code for every crate through LLVM; it emits device 161 artifacts only for the listed owner crates. 162 163 The filter controls compilation, not runtime module names. Code in an excluded 164 crate or target must not call that target's generated `load()` function. In a 165 package with several targets, embedded bundles still use the package name, so 166 an excluded target's loader could otherwise find a selected sibling target's 167 bundle. 168 169 Passthrough builds include the effective codegen settings and backend build in 170 Cargo's rustc fingerprint. Changing the target, output mode, FMA policy, owner 171 list, configured codegen environment, or backend `.so` therefore regenerates 172 device artifacts instead of reusing a stale Cargo result. 173 174 ### `cargo oxide emit-ltoir ` 175 176 Compiles a crate's device code to a binary LTOIR artifact in one step, for the 177 Tile-to-SIMT interop workflow ([#96](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/issues/96)): 178 cuda-oxide is the SIMT participant, producing LTOIR that a tile or CUDA C++ kernel 179 links against. It builds the crate in NVVM IR mode, then runs the emitted 180 `.ll` through libNVVM `-gen-lto`, writing `.ltoir`. 181 182 `--arch` is required, since LTOIR is architecture-specific. It accepts `sm_XX`, 183 `compute_XX`, or a bare `XX`. The default output path is `/.ltoir`; 184 `-o/--output` overrides it. 185 186 ```bash 187 cargo oxide emit-ltoir standalone_device_fn --arch sm_100 188 cargo oxide emit-ltoir my_simt_crate --arch sm_120 -o build/simt.ltoir 189 ``` 190 191 cuda-oxide chooses the NVVM IR format from the selected architecture. 192 Pre-Blackwell targets use LLVM 7 typed pointers; `compute_100` and newer targets 193 use modern opaque pointers. The target is checked before export and recorded 194 alongside the artifact so the runtime uses the same format. 195 196 ### `cargo oxide test` 197 198 Runs `cargo test` through the cuda-oxide backend. With no extra arguments it 199 runs the workspace's normal test selection. Put Cargo test arguments after 200 `--`, including the test-binary separator when needed. 201 202 ```bash 203 cargo oxide test 204 cargo oxide test -- -p my_app --release --test gpu_smoke -- --nocapture 205 cargo oxide test --device-codegen-crate gpu_smoke -- -p my_app --test gpu_smoke 206 ``` 207 208 ### `cargo oxide pipeline ` 209 210 Shows verbose progress plus the selected IR artifacts: MIR collection, 211 `dialect-mir` before and after `mem2reg`, LLVM dialect, LLVM IR, and final PTX. 212 213 ```bash 214 cargo oxide pipeline vecadd 215 cargo oxide pipeline device_ffi_test --emit-nvvm-ir --arch sm_120 216 ``` 217 218 ### `cargo oxide debug ` 219 220 Builds with debug info (`-C debuginfo=2`) and launches cuda-gdb. Supports 221 `--tui` for GDB's TUI mode and `--cgdb` for the cgdb frontend. 222 223 `debug` uses Cargo's reported executable artifact, so custom binary names, 224 `package.default-run`, configured target directories, host target triples, and 225 virtual workspaces do not require path guessing. Use `--bin ` when a 226 package has multiple binaries and no unambiguous default. 227 228 ### `cargo oxide new [--async]` 229 230 Scaffolds a new standalone cuda-oxide project with `Cargo.toml`, `rust-toolchain.toml`, and a working `src/main.rs` containing a vector addition kernel. The default template uses `#[cuda_module]` with typed synchronous launch methods; `--async` generates a template with `tokio`, `cuda-async`, and typed lazy `DeviceOperation` launches. 231 232 ```bash 233 cargo oxide new my_kernel 234 cd my_kernel 235 cargo oxide run 236 ``` 237 238 ### `cargo oxide fmt [--check]` 239 240 Formats all crates in the workspace: root workspace, `rustc-codegen-cuda`, and all examples. With `--check`, reports files that need formatting without modifying them. 241 242 ### `cargo oxide doctor` 243 244 Validates that your environment is correctly set up: Rust nightly toolchain, 245 CUDA headers (`cuda.h`), CUDA toolkit (`nvcc`, libNVVM, nvJitLink, 246 libdevice), LLVM (`llc`), clang/libclang, the NVIDIA driver / GPU, and the 247 codegen backend `.so`. Every check reports what was found or how to fix it. 248 249 `cargo-oxide` itself builds and runs without the CUDA toolkit and without an 250 NVIDIA driver, and `doctor` never builds anything first, so it works on a 251 bare machine and tells you exactly what is missing. The driver / GPU check is 252 informational (only `cargo oxide run` needs a GPU), and a missing backend 253 `.so` just points at `cargo oxide setup` (`run`/`build` build it on demand 254 anyway). 255 256 ### `cargo oxide setup` 257 258 Explicitly builds (or rebuilds) the codegen backend. Normally this happens automatically on every `run`/`build`/`pipeline` command, but `setup` is useful after pulling new changes or for CI. 259 260 ## Backend Discovery 261 262 When `cargo oxide` needs the `librustc_codegen_cuda.so` backend, it searches in this order: 263 264 1. **`CUDA_OXIDE_BACKEND` env var** — explicit path override 265 2. **Project config** — `.cargo/cuda-oxide.toml` 266 3. **Local repo** — detects `crates/rustc-codegen-cuda` relative to workspace root, builds from source 267 4. **Cached `.so`** — checks `~/.cargo/cuda-oxide/librustc_codegen_cuda.so` 268 5. **Auto-fetch** — clones the cuda-oxide repo, builds, and caches (one-time) 269 270 Project config can also provide the default architecture, extra rustc flags, 271 and child-process environment: 272 273 ```toml 274 backend = "/path/to/librustc_codegen_cuda.so" 275 default-arch = "sm_86" 276 extra-rustflags = ["--cfg", "my_device_cfg"] 277 278 [env] 279 MY_BUILD_FLAG = "1" 280 ``` 281 282 Relative backend paths are resolved from the `.cargo` directory containing the 283 config file. Each `extra-rustflags` array element remains one rustc argument, 284 including values that contain spaces. 285 286 Configuration values are defaults. Precedence is: 287 288 1. explicit `cargo oxide` flags and internal artifact paths; 289 2. environment variables inherited by `cargo oxide`; 290 3. `.cargo/cuda-oxide.toml` defaults. 291 292 Do not put `RUSTFLAGS` or `CARGO_ENCODED_RUSTFLAGS` in the `[env]` table; use 293 `extra-rustflags` for project defaults. cargo-oxide combines configured flags, 294 inherited user flags, explicit `--device-cfg` values, and its required compiler 295 flags in boundary-preserving `CARGO_ENCODED_RUSTFLAGS`. Required compiler flags 296 are applied last so inherited settings cannot replace the cuda-oxide backend or 297 disable its correctness-critical codegen options. 298 299 ## Architecture 300 301 ```text 302 crates/cargo-oxide/ 303 ├── Cargo.toml 304 └── src/ 305 ├── main.rs # CLI definitions (clap) + dispatch 306 ├── backend.rs # Backend discovery + build logic 307 └── commands.rs # All command implementations 308 ``` 309 310 ## Future Commands 311 312 | Command | Description | 313 |---------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------| 314 | `cargo oxide bench ` | GPU profiling (nsys/ncu integration), report TFLOPS | 315 | `cargo oxide clean` | Remove generated PTX/LL/LTOIR artifacts and build caches| 316 | `cargo oxide update` | Update the cached codegen backend to latest version | 317 | `cargo oxide list` | List examples with descriptions and hardware reqs | 318 | `cargo oxide inspect ` | Show generated PTX without the full pipeline dump | 319

# cargo-oxide Cargo subcommand for building and running Rust GPU programs with cuda-oxide. Replaces the previous `xtask` pattern with a proper cargo subcommand that works both inside the cuda-oxide repo (for developers) and externally (for users who `cargo install`). ## Installation **Internal developers** (inside the cuda-oxide repo): no installation needed. The workspace alias makes `cargo oxide` work immediately. **External users**: Install with the project's pinned nightly toolchain: ```bash cargo +nightly-2026-04-03 install --git https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide.git cargo-oxide ``` On first run, `cargo-oxide` will automatically fetch and build the codegen backend if it's not already available. ## Usage ```bash cargo oxide new my_project # scaffold a new cuda-oxide project cargo oxide new my_project --async # scaffold with async template (tokio + cuda-async) cargo oxide run vecadd # build + run an example cargo oxide build vecadd # compile only (no run) cargo oxide emit-ltoir vecadd --arch sm_100 # device code -> .ltoir (Tile/SIMT interop) cargo oxide build -- -p my_app # arbitrary cargo build through cuda-oxide cargo oxide test # cargo test with cuda-oxide defaults cargo oxide test -- -p my_app # arbitrary cargo test through cuda-oxide cargo oxide pipeline vecadd # verbose pipeline dump # (MIR -> dialect-mir -> LLVM dialect -> LLVM IR -> PTX) cargo oxide sanitize vecadd # build + run under NVIDIA Compute Sanitizer cargo oxide debug vecadd --tui # build + launch cuda-gdb cargo oxide fmt # format all crates cargo oxide fmt --check # check formatting cargo oxide doctor # validate environment cargo oxide setup # explicitly build the codegen backend ``` ### Flags | Flag | Applies to | Description | |------------------------------|----------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------| | `--emit-nvvm-ir` | run, build, pipeline | Generate NVVM IR for libNVVM | | `--arch ` | run, sanitize, build, test, pipeline, emit-ltoir | Target architecture override | | `--features ` | run, sanitize, debug, build, build passthrough, emit-ltoir | Comma-separated cargo features to enable | | `--bin ` | run, sanitize, debug | Specific binary target to build/run | | `--tool ` | sanitize | Compute Sanitizer tool: `memcheck`, `racecheck`, `initcheck`, or `synccheck` | | `-o, --output

` | emit-ltoir | Output path for the `.ltoir` artifact | | `--cargo-target-dir ` | build/test passthrough | Cargo target directory | | `--device-codegen-crate ` | build/test passthrough | Comma-separated device owner crate filter | | `--device-cfg ` | build/test passthrough | Append `--cfg NAME` to rustflags | | `-v, --verbose` | run, sanitize, build, test, emit-ltoir | Show detailed compilation output | | `--no-fmad` | run, sanitize, build, emit-ltoir, pipeline | Keep ordinary multiply and add/subtract operations separate | | `--async` | new | Use the async template | | `--cgdb` | debug | Use cgdb instead of cuda-gdb | | `--tui` | debug | Use GDB's TUI interface | | `--check` | fmt | Check formatting only | `--arch` is required for `emit-ltoir` and explicit NVVM IR output because those artifacts are architecture-specific. Without an override, `run` detects the local GPU, while `build` and `pipeline` use the compiler's feature-based target so they remain useful for cross-compilation. `--no-fmad` (or `CUDA_OXIDE_NO_FMA=1`) disables implicit contraction of an ordinary multiply followed by an add or subtract, so the two operations round separately. Explicit fused operations such as `f32::mul_add` remain fused. For NVVM IR and LTOIR, cuda-oxide records the policy in matching `.options` and versioned `.target` files and passes `-fma=0` through libNVVM and nvJitLink. Keep both sidecars with the artifact when another build system consumes it. ## Commands ### `cargo oxide run ` Builds the codegen backend, compiles the example with the custom backend, and runs it. This is the primary command for day-to-day development. When neither `--arch` nor `CUDA_OXIDE_TARGET` is set, `run` detects the compute capability of CUDA device 0 and targets that architecture so the generated PTX can load on the local GPU. Use `--arch ` or `CUDA_OXIDE_TARGET=` to override this for a specific device or cross-target workflow. ```bash cargo oxide run vecadd cargo oxide run gemm_sol cargo oxide run device_ffi_test --emit-nvvm-ir --arch sm_120 cargo oxide run cutile_inter_kernel ``` Interop examples can declare extra cuda-oxide device crates with `[[package.metadata.cuda-oxide.device-crates]]`, plus optional `[package.metadata.cuda-oxide.interop]` metadata. `cargo oxide run` builds those device crates with `rustc-codegen-cuda`, writes their PTX to the configured location, and then builds/runs the host crate normally. `cutile_inter_kernel` uses this path: the host crate is a cutile-rs program, while `simt/` is a cuda-oxide SIMT PTX crate loaded by the host at runtime. ### `cargo oxide sanitize ` Builds an example like `cargo oxide run`, then executes the produced release binary under NVIDIA Compute Sanitizer. `memcheck` is the default tool; use `--tool racecheck`, `--tool initcheck`, or `--tool synccheck` for shared-memory hazard, uninitialized global-memory, or synchronization checks. ```bash cargo oxide sanitize vecadd --arch sm_75 cargo oxide sanitize sharedmem --tool racecheck cargo oxide sanitize debug --tool synccheck -- --kernel-name kns=sync cargo oxide sanitize vecadd -- --leak-check full cargo oxide sanitize my_app -- --leak-check full -- --app-flag value ``` Arguments after the first `--` are passed to `compute-sanitizer` before the executable. A second `--` splits target program arguments, matching `compute-sanitizer [options] [your-program] [your-program-options]`. `sanitize` uses Cargo's reported executable artifact, so custom binary names, workspace layouts, configured target directories, and host target triples do not require path guessing. Device line tables are enabled by default while normal optimization remains on. Compute Sanitizer's own error exit code defaults to zero. This command supplies `--error-exitcode 86` unless you pass an explicit value, so tool findings fail scripts and CI. NVIDIA recommends treating the tools as complementary: `racecheck`, `initcheck`, and `synccheck` do not replace `memcheck` memory-access checking. ### `cargo oxide build ` Same as `run` but stops after compilation. Useful for examples that require hardware you don't have (e.g., Blackwell tensor cores). ```bash cargo oxide build htens # compiles PTX, doesn't try to run on GPU cargo oxide build tcgen05 # sm_100a only, but PTX generation works anywhere ``` `build` also has a passthrough mode for normal Cargo workspaces. Put the Cargo arguments after `--`; cargo-oxide supplies the backend, target architecture, configured environment, and optional device owner filters. ```bash cargo oxide build -- # plain `cargo build` cargo oxide build --arch sm_86 -- -p my_app --bin app --release cargo oxide build --cargo-target-dir target/cuda -- -p my_app --release cargo oxide build --device-codegen-crate gpu-kernels,math_gpu -- -p my_app ``` The owner filter is matched against rustc crate names, so Cargo package hyphens are normalized to underscores (`gpu-kernels` matches `gpu_kernels`). The CUDA backend still compiles host code for every crate through LLVM; it emits device artifacts only for the listed owner crates. The filter controls compilation, not runtime module names. Code in an excluded crate or target must not call that target's generated `load()` function. In a package with several targets, embedded bundles still use the package name, so an excluded target's loader could otherwise find a selected sibling target's bundle. Passthrough builds include the effective codegen settings and backend build in Cargo's rustc fingerprint. Changing the target, output mode, FMA policy, owner list, configured codegen environment, or backend `.so` therefore regenerates device artifacts instead of reusing a stale Cargo result. ### `cargo oxide emit-ltoir ` Compiles a crate's device code to a binary LTOIR artifact in one step, for the Tile-to-SIMT interop workflow ([#96](https://github.com/NVlabs/cuda-oxide/issues/96)): cuda-oxide is the SIMT participant, producing LTOIR that a tile or CUDA C++ kernel links against. It builds the crate in NVVM IR mode, then runs the emitted `.ll` through libNVVM `-gen-lto`, writing `.ltoir`. `--arch` is required, since LTOIR is architecture-specific. It accepts `sm_XX`, `compute_XX`, or a bare `XX`. The default output path is `/.ltoir`; `-o/--output` overrides it. ```bash cargo oxide emit-ltoir standalone_device_fn --arch sm_100 cargo oxide emit-ltoir my_simt_crate --arch sm_120 -o build/simt.ltoir ``` cuda-oxide chooses the NVVM IR format from the selected architecture. Pre-Blackwell targets use LLVM 7 typed pointers; `compute_100` and newer targets use modern opaque pointers. The target is checked before export and recorded alongside the artifact so the runtime uses the same format. ### `cargo oxide test` Runs `cargo test` through the cuda-oxide backend. With no extra arguments it runs the workspace's normal test selection. Put Cargo test arguments after `--`, including the test-binary separator when needed. ```bash cargo oxide test cargo oxide test -- -p my_app --release --test gpu_smoke -- --nocapture cargo oxide test --device-codegen-crate gpu_smoke -- -p my_app --test gpu_smoke ``` ### `cargo oxide pipeline ` Shows verbose progress plus the selected IR artifacts: MIR collection, `dialect-mir` before and after `mem2reg`, LLVM dialect, LLVM IR, and final PTX. ```bash cargo oxide pipeline vecadd cargo oxide pipeline device_ffi_test --emit-nvvm-ir --arch sm_120 ``` ### `cargo oxide debug ` Builds with debug info (`-C debuginfo=2`) and launches cuda-gdb. Supports `--tui` for GDB's TUI mode and `--cgdb` for the cgdb frontend. `debug` uses Cargo's reported executable artifact, so custom binary names, `package.default-run`, configured target directories, host target triples, and virtual workspaces do not require path guessing. Use `--bin ` when a package has multiple binaries and no unambiguous default. ### `cargo oxide new [--async]` Scaffolds a new standalone cuda-oxide project with `Cargo.toml`, `rust-toolchain.toml`, and a working `src/main.rs` containing a vector addition kernel. The default template uses `#[cuda_module]` with typed synchronous launch methods; `--async` generates a template with `tokio`, `cuda-async`, and typed lazy `DeviceOperation` launches. ```bash cargo oxide new my_kernel cd my_kernel cargo oxide run ``` ### `cargo oxide fmt [--check]` Formats all crates in the workspace: root workspace, `rustc-codegen-cuda`, and all examples. With `--check`, reports files that need formatting without modifying them. ### `cargo oxide doctor` Validates that your environment is correctly set up: Rust nightly toolchain, CUDA headers (`cuda.h`), CUDA toolkit (`nvcc`, libNVVM, nvJitLink, libdevice), LLVM (`llc`), clang/libclang, the NVIDIA driver / GPU, and the codegen backend `.so`. Every check reports what was found or how to fix it. `cargo-oxide` itself builds and runs without the CUDA toolkit and without an NVIDIA driver, and `doctor` never builds anything first, so it works on a bare machine and tells you exactly what is missing. The driver / GPU check is informational (only `cargo oxide run` needs a GPU), and a missing backend `.so` just points at `cargo oxide setup` (`run`/`build` build it on demand anyway). ### `cargo oxide setup` Explicitly builds (or rebuilds) the codegen backend. Normally this happens automatically on every `run`/`build`/`pipeline` command, but `setup` is useful after pulling new changes or for CI. ## Backend Discovery When `cargo oxide` needs the `librustc_codegen_cuda.so` backend, it searches in this order: 1. **`CUDA_OXIDE_BACKEND` env var** — explicit path override 2. **Project config** — `.cargo/cuda-oxide.toml` 3. **Local repo** — detects `crates/rustc-codegen-cuda` relative to workspace root, builds from source 4. **Cached `.so`** — checks `~/.cargo/cuda-oxide/librustc_codegen_cuda.so` 5. **Auto-fetch** — clones the cuda-oxide repo, builds, and caches (one-time) Project config can also provide the default architecture, extra rustc flags, and child-process environment: ```toml backend = "/path/to/librustc_codegen_cuda.so" default-arch = "sm_86" extra-rustflags = ["--cfg", "my_device_cfg"] [env] MY_BUILD_FLAG = "1" ``` Relative backend paths are resolved from the `.cargo` directory containing the config file. Each `extra-rustflags` array element remains one rustc argument, including values that contain spaces. Configuration values are defaults. Precedence is: 1. explicit `cargo oxide` flags and internal artifact paths; 2. environment variables inherited by `cargo oxide`; 3. `.cargo/cuda-oxide.toml` defaults. Do not put `RUSTFLAGS` or `CARGO_ENCODED_RUSTFLAGS` in the `[env]` table; use `extra-rustflags` for project defaults. cargo-oxide combines configured flags, inherited user flags, explicit `--device-cfg` values, and its required compiler flags in boundary-preserving `CARGO_ENCODED_RUSTFLAGS`. Required compiler flags are applied last so inherited settings cannot replace the cuda-oxide backend or disable its correctness-critical codegen options. ## Architecture ```text crates/cargo-oxide/ ├── Cargo.toml └── src/ ├── main.rs # CLI definitions (clap) + dispatch ├── backend.rs # Backend discovery + build logic └── commands.rs # All command implementations ``` ## Future Commands | Command | Description | |---------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------| | `cargo oxide bench ` | GPU profiling (nsys/ncu integration), report TFLOPS | | `cargo oxide clean` | Remove generated PTX/LL/LTOIR artifacts and build caches| | `cargo oxide update` | Update the cached codegen backend to latest version | | `cargo oxide list` | List examples with descriptions and hardware reqs | | `cargo oxide inspect ` | Show generated PTX without the full pipeline dump |

1	/*

5	
6	//! cargo-oxide: Cargo subcommand for building and running cuda-oxide programs.
7	//!
8	//! Replaces the xtask pattern with a proper cargo subcommand that works both
9	//! inside the cuda-oxide repo (for developers) and externally (for users).
10	//!
11	//! # Usage
12	//!
13	//! ```bash
14	//! cargo oxide run vecadd              # build + run an example
15	//! cargo oxide build vecadd            # build only
16	//! cargo oxide pipeline vecadd         # verbose pipeline dump
17	//! cargo oxide sanitize vecadd         # run under NVIDIA Compute Sanitizer
18	//! cargo oxide debug vecadd --tui      # build + cuda-gdb
19	//! cargo oxide new my_kernel            # scaffold a standalone project
20	//! cargo oxide new my_kernel --async   # scaffold with async template
21	//! cargo oxide fmt                     # format all crates
22	//! cargo oxide doctor                  # check environment
23	//! cargo oxide setup                   # explicitly build/install backend
24	//! ```
25	
26	use clap::{Parser, Subcommand, ValueEnum};
27	use std::path::PathBuf;
28	
29	mod backend;
30	mod commands;
31	
32	/// Top-level CLI structure parsed by clap.
33	///
34	/// The binary is named `cargo-oxide` so that `cargo oxide <subcommand>` works
35	/// as a cargo subcommand. The workspace alias in `.cargo/config.toml` also
36	/// routes `cargo oxide` here when run inside the repo.
37	#[derive(Parser)]
38	#[command(
39	    name = "cargo-oxide",
40	    bin_name = "cargo oxide",
41	    about = "Build and run Rust GPU programs with cuda-oxide",
42	    version
43	)]
44	struct Cli {
45	    #[command(subcommand)]
46	    command: Commands,
47	}
48	
49	/// Available subcommands for `cargo oxide`.
50	#[derive(Subcommand)]
51	enum Commands {
52	    /// Build and run an example or project
53	    Run {
54	        /// Example name (required in workspace, optional for standalone projects)
55	        example: Option<String>,
56	        /// Generate NVVM IR (use with libNVVM -gen-lto)
57	        #[arg(long)]
58	        emit_nvvm_ir: bool,
59	        /// Target architecture (e.g., sm_90, sm_100, sm_120). When omitted,
60	        /// `run` auto-detects the compute capability of CUDA device 0 so the
61	        /// generated module loads on the local GPU; set `CUDA_OXIDE_TARGET`
62	        /// in the environment for a non-interactive override.
63	        #[arg(long)]
64	        arch: Option<String>,
65	        /// Comma-separated list of features to enable
66	        #[arg(long)]
67	        features: Option<String>,
68	        /// Pick a specific binary in a multi-bin package (forwarded as
69	        /// `cargo run --bin <name>`). Defaults to the package's
70	        /// `default-run`.
71	        #[arg(long)]
72	        bin: Option<String>,
73	        /// Show verbose compilation output
74	        #[arg(short, long)]
75	        verbose: bool,
76	        /// Disable FMA contraction (default: on, matching nvcc --fmad=true).
77	        /// Also settable via CUDA_OXIDE_NO_FMA=1.
78	        #[arg(long)]
79	        no_fmad: bool,
80	    },
81	    /// Build and run an example or project under NVIDIA Compute Sanitizer
82	    Sanitize {
83	        /// Example name (required in workspace, optional for standalone projects)
84	        example: Option<String>,
85	        /// Compute Sanitizer tool to run
86	        #[arg(long, value_enum, default_value_t = SanitizerTool::Memcheck)]
87	        tool: SanitizerTool,
88	        /// Target architecture (e.g., sm_90, sm_100, sm_120). When omitted,
89	        /// `sanitize` uses the same local-GPU target detection as `run`.
90	        #[arg(long)]
91	        arch: Option<String>,
92	        /// Comma-separated list of features to enable
93	        #[arg(long)]
94	        features: Option<String>,
95	        /// Pick a specific binary in a multi-bin package
96	        #[arg(long)]
97	        bin: Option<String>,
98	        /// Show verbose compilation output
99	        #[arg(short, long)]
100	        verbose: bool,
101	        /// Disable implicit FMA contraction in device codegen.
102	        /// Also settable via CUDA_OXIDE_NO_FMA=1.
103	        #[arg(long)]
104	        no_fmad: bool,
105	        /// Additional arguments passed to compute-sanitizer before the binary.
106	        /// Use a second `--` inside this list to pass arguments to the target
107	        /// program after the binary.
108	        #[arg(last = true, num_args = 0.., allow_hyphen_values = true)]
109	        sanitizer_args: Vec<String>,
110	    },
111	    /// Build an example or project (compile only, don't run)
112	    Build {
113	        /// Example name (required in workspace, optional for standalone projects)
114	        example: Option<String>,
115	        /// Generate NVVM IR (use with libNVVM -gen-lto)
116	        #[arg(long)]
117	        emit_nvvm_ir: bool,
118	        /// Target architecture (e.g., sm_90, sm_100, sm_120)
119	        #[arg(long)]
120	        arch: Option<String>,
121	        /// Comma-separated list of features to enable
122	        #[arg(long)]
123	        features: Option<String>,
124	        /// Show verbose compilation output
125	        #[arg(short, long)]
126	        verbose: bool,
127	        /// Disable FMA contraction (default: on, matching nvcc --fmad=true).
128	        /// Also settable via CUDA_OXIDE_NO_FMA=1.
129	        #[arg(long)]
130	        no_fmad: bool,
131	        /// Cargo target directory for passthrough mode
132	        #[arg(long)]
133	        cargo_target_dir: Option<PathBuf>,
134	        /// Comma-separated cuda-oxide owner crate filter for device codegen
135	        #[arg(long)]
136	        device_codegen_crate: Option<String>,
137	        /// Repeatable cfg appended as `--cfg NAME` for passthrough device codegen
138	        #[arg(long = "device-cfg")]
139	        device_cfgs: Vec<String>,
140	        /// Cargo build arguments for passthrough mode. Use after `--`.
141	        #[arg(last = true, num_args = 0.., allow_hyphen_values = true)]
142	        cargo_args: Vec<String>,
143	    },
144	    /// Run Cargo tests through the cuda-oxide backend
145	    Test {
146	        /// Target architecture (e.g., sm_90, sm_100, sm_120)
147	        #[arg(long)]
148	        arch: Option<String>,
149	        /// Cargo target directory
150	        #[arg(long)]
151	        cargo_target_dir: Option<PathBuf>,
152	        /// Comma-separated cuda-oxide owner crate filter for device codegen
153	        #[arg(long)]
154	        device_codegen_crate: Option<String>,
155	        /// Repeatable cfg appended as `--cfg NAME` for device codegen
156	        #[arg(long = "device-cfg")]
157	        device_cfgs: Vec<String>,
158	        /// Show verbose compilation output
159	        #[arg(short, long)]
160	        verbose: bool,
161	        /// Cargo test arguments. Use after `--`; empty runs plain `cargo test`.
162	        #[arg(last = true, num_args = 0.., allow_hyphen_values = true)]
163	        cargo_args: Vec<String>,
164	    },
165	    /// Compile a crate's device code to a binary LTOIR artifact in one step.
166	    ///
167	    /// Produces the SIMT artifact a tile or C++ kernel links against
168	    /// (NVVM IR emission followed by libNVVM `-gen-lto`), writing
169	    /// `<crate>.ltoir` plus target/options sidecars. See the Tile-to-SIMT
170	    /// interop tracker (#96).
171	    EmitLtoir {
172	        /// Crate name (required in workspace, optional for standalone projects)
173	        example: Option<String>,
174	        /// Target architecture (e.g. sm_90, sm_100, sm_120). Required: LTOIR is
175	        /// architecture-specific.
176	        #[arg(long)]
177	        arch: String,
178	        /// Comma-separated list of features to enable
179	        #[arg(long)]
180	        features: Option<String>,
181	        /// Output path for the `.ltoir` file (default: `<crate-dir>/<crate>.ltoir`)
182	        #[arg(long, short)]
183	        output: Option<PathBuf>,
184	        /// Show verbose compilation output
185	        #[arg(short, long)]
186	        verbose: bool,
187	        /// Disable implicit FMA contraction in both libNVVM and nvJitLink.
188	        /// Also settable via CUDA_OXIDE_NO_FMA=1.
189	        #[arg(long)]
190	        no_fmad: bool,
191	    },
192	    /// Show the full compilation pipeline (MIR -> PTX/NVVM IR) with verbose output
193	    Pipeline {
194	        /// Example name (required in workspace, optional for standalone projects)
195	        example: Option<String>,
196	        /// Generate NVVM IR (use with libNVVM -gen-lto)
197	        #[arg(long)]
198	        emit_nvvm_ir: bool,
199	        /// Target architecture (e.g., sm_90, sm_100, sm_120)
200	        #[arg(long)]
201	        arch: Option<String>,
202	        /// Disable FMA contraction (default: on, matching nvcc --fmad=true).
203	        /// Also settable via CUDA_OXIDE_NO_FMA=1.
204	        #[arg(long)]
205	        no_fmad: bool,
206	    },
207	    /// Build with debug info and launch cuda-gdb
208	    Debug {
209	        /// Example name (required in workspace, optional for standalone projects)
210	        example: Option<String>,
211	        /// Target architecture (e.g., sm_90, sm_100, sm_120). When omitted,
212	        /// `debug` auto-detects the compute capability of CUDA device 0 so the
213	        /// generated module loads on the local GPU; set `CUDA_OXIDE_TARGET`
214	        /// in the environment for a non-interactive override.
215	        #[arg(long)]
216	        arch: Option<String>,
217	        /// Cargo features to enable
218	        #[arg(long)]
219	        features: Option<String>,
220	        /// Specific binary target to build and debug
221	        #[arg(long)]
222	        bin: Option<String>,
223	        /// Use cgdb frontend (better source view, vim keys)
224	        #[arg(long)]
225	        cgdb: bool,
226	        /// Use GDB's built-in TUI interface
227	        #[arg(long)]
228	        tui: bool,
229	    },
230	    /// Format all crates (root workspace, codegen backend, examples)
231	    Fmt {
232	        /// Check formatting without modifying files
233	        #[arg(long)]
234	        check: bool,
235	    },
236	    /// Scaffold a new standalone cuda-oxide project
237	    New {
238	        /// Project name (becomes directory name and package name)
239	        name: String,
240	        /// Use async template (tokio + cuda-async + DeviceOperation)
241	        #[arg(long = "async")]
242	        async_mode: bool,
243	    },
244	    /// Check that your environment is set up correctly
245	    Doctor,
246	    /// Build and cache the codegen backend
247	    Setup,
248	}
249	
250	#[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, PartialEq, Eq, ValueEnum)]
251	enum SanitizerTool {
252	    Memcheck,
253	    Racecheck,
254	    Initcheck,
255	    Synccheck,
256	}
257	
258	impl SanitizerTool {
259	    fn as_str(self) -> &'static str {
260	        match self {
261	            Self::Memcheck => "memcheck",
262	            Self::Racecheck => "racecheck",
263	            Self::Initcheck => "initcheck",
264	            Self::Synccheck => "synccheck",
265	        }
266	    }
267	}
268	
269	fn split_sanitizer_and_application_args(args: &[String]) -> (Vec<String>, Vec<String>) {
270	    match args.iter().position(|arg| arg == "--") {
271	        Some(separator) => (args[..separator].to_vec(), args[separator + 1..].to_vec()),
272	        None => (args.to_vec(), Vec::new()),
273	    }
274	}
275	
276	fn has_passthrough_separator(args: &[String]) -> bool {
277	    args.iter().skip(2).any(|arg| arg == "--")
278	}
279	
280	fn use_build_passthrough(
281	    explicit_separator: bool,
282	    cargo_target_dir_is_set: bool,
283	    owner_filter_is_set: bool,
284	    has_device_cfgs: bool,
285	    has_cargo_args: bool,
286	) -> bool {
287	    explicit_separator
288	        || cargo_target_dir_is_set
289	        || owner_filter_is_set
290	        || has_device_cfgs
291	        || has_cargo_args
292	}
293	
294	fn main() {
295	    // Handle both invocation methods:
296	    // 1. Cargo subcommand: `cargo oxide run vecadd` → argv = ["cargo-oxide", "oxide", "run", "vecadd"]
297	    // 2. Cargo alias:      `cargo oxide run vecadd` → argv = ["target/.../cargo-oxide", "run", "vecadd"]
298	    let args: Vec<String> = std::env::args().collect();
299	    let effective_args = if args.get(1).map(|s| s.as_str()) == Some("oxide") {
300	        let mut filtered = vec![args[0].clone()];
301	        filtered.extend(args[2..].iter().cloned());
302	        filtered
303	    } else {
304	        args
305	    };
306	
307	    let explicit_passthrough = has_passthrough_separator(&effective_args);
308	    let cli = Cli::parse_from(effective_args);
309	
310	    match cli.command {
311	        Commands::Run {
312	            example,
313	            emit_nvvm_ir,
314	            arch,
315	            features,
316	            bin,
317	            verbose,
318	            no_fmad,
319	        } => {
320	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
321	            let example = resolve_example_name(example, &ctx, "run");
322	            validate_nvvm_ir_arch(&ctx, &example, emit_nvvm_ir, arch.as_deref());
323	            commands::codegen_run(
324	                &ctx,
325	                &example,
326	                verbose,
327	                emit_nvvm_ir,
328	                arch.as_deref(),
329	                features.as_deref(),
330	                bin.as_deref(),
331	                no_fmad,
332	            );
333	        }
334	        Commands::Sanitize {
335	            example,
336	            tool,
337	            arch,
338	            features,
339	            bin,
340	            verbose,
341	            no_fmad,
342	            sanitizer_args,
343	        } => {
344	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
345	            let example = resolve_example_name(example, &ctx, "sanitize");
346	            let (sanitizer_args, application_args) =
347	                split_sanitizer_and_application_args(&sanitizer_args);
348	            commands::codegen_sanitize(
349	                &ctx,
350	                &example,
351	                tool.as_str(),
352	                &sanitizer_args,
353	                &application_args,
354	                verbose,
355	                arch.as_deref(),
356	                features.as_deref(),
357	                bin.as_deref(),
358	                no_fmad,
359	            );
360	        }
361	        Commands::Build {
362	            example,
363	            emit_nvvm_ir,
364	            arch,
365	            features,
366	            verbose,
367	            no_fmad,
368	            cargo_target_dir,
369	            device_codegen_crate,
370	            device_cfgs,
371	            cargo_args,
372	        } => {
373	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
374	            let passthrough = use_build_passthrough(
375	                explicit_passthrough,
376	                cargo_target_dir.is_some(),
377	                device_codegen_crate.is_some(),
378	                !device_cfgs.is_empty(),
379	                !cargo_args.is_empty(),
380	            );
381	            if !passthrough {
382	                let example = resolve_example_name(example, &ctx, "build");
383	                validate_nvvm_ir_arch(&ctx, &example, emit_nvvm_ir, arch.as_deref());
384	                commands::codegen_build(
385	                    &ctx,
386	                    &example,
387	                    verbose,
388	                    emit_nvvm_ir,
389	                    arch.as_deref(),
390	                    features.as_deref(),
391	                    no_fmad,
392	                );
393	            } else {
394	                if example.is_some() {
395	                    eprintln!(
396	                        "Error: `cargo oxide build` accepts either an example name or passthrough args after `--`, not both"
397	                    );
398	                    std::process::exit(2);
399	                }
400	                validate_nvvm_ir_arch(&ctx, "cargo build", emit_nvvm_ir, arch.as_deref());
401	                commands::codegen_cargo_passthrough(
402	                    &ctx,
403	                    "build",
404	                    commands::CargoPassthroughOptions {
405	                        verbose,
406	                        emit_nvvm_ir,
407	                        arch: arch.as_deref(),
408	                        features: features.as_deref(),
409	                        cargo_target_dir: cargo_target_dir.as_deref(),
410	                        device_codegen_crate: device_codegen_crate.as_deref(),
411	                        device_cfgs: &device_cfgs,
412	                        no_fmad,
413	                    },
414	                    &cargo_args,
415	                );
416	            }
417	        }
418	        Commands::Test {
419	            arch,
420	            cargo_target_dir,
421	            device_codegen_crate,
422	            device_cfgs,
423	            verbose,
424	            cargo_args,
425	        } => {
426	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
427	            commands::codegen_cargo_passthrough(
428	                &ctx,
429	                "test",
430	                commands::CargoPassthroughOptions {
431	                    verbose,
432	                    emit_nvvm_ir: false,
433	                    arch: arch.as_deref(),
434	                    features: None,
435	                    cargo_target_dir: cargo_target_dir.as_deref(),
436	                    device_codegen_crate: device_codegen_crate.as_deref(),
437	                    device_cfgs: &device_cfgs,
438	                    no_fmad: false,
439	                },
440	                &cargo_args,
441	            );
442	        }
443	        Commands::EmitLtoir {
444	            example,
445	            arch,
446	            features,
447	            output,
448	            verbose,
449	            no_fmad,
450	        } => {
451	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
452	            let example = resolve_example_name(example, &ctx, "emit-ltoir");
453	            commands::emit_ltoir(
454	                &ctx,
455	                &example,
456	                &arch,
457	                features.as_deref(),
458	                output.as_deref(),
459	                verbose,
460	                no_fmad,
461	            );
462	        }
463	        Commands::Pipeline {
464	            example,
465	            emit_nvvm_ir,
466	            arch,
467	            no_fmad,
468	        } => {
469	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
470	            let example = resolve_example_name(example, &ctx, "pipeline");
471	            validate_nvvm_ir_arch(&ctx, &example, emit_nvvm_ir, arch.as_deref());
472	            commands::codegen_show_pipeline(&ctx, &example, emit_nvvm_ir, arch.as_deref(), no_fmad);
473	        }
474	        Commands::Debug {
475	            example,
476	            arch,
477	            features,
478	            bin,
479	            cgdb,
480	            tui,
481	        } => {
482	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
483	            let example = resolve_example_name(example, &ctx, "debug");
484	            commands::codegen_debug(
485	                &ctx,
486	                &example,
487	                arch.as_deref(),
488	                features.as_deref(),
489	                bin.as_deref(),
490	                cgdb,
491	                tui,
492	            );
493	        }
494	        Commands::Fmt { check } => {
495	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
496	            commands::format_all(&ctx, check);
497	        }
498	        Commands::New { name, async_mode } => {
499	            commands::scaffold_new(&name, async_mode);
500	        }
501	        Commands::Doctor => {
502	            // Side-effect-free resolver: doctor must never build the backend
503	            // (or clone anything) before it can diagnose the environment.
504	            let ctx = commands::resolve_doctor_context();
505	            commands::doctor(&ctx);
506	        }
507	        Commands::Setup => {
508	            let ctx = commands::resolve_context();
509	            commands::setup(&ctx);
510	        }
511	    }
512	}
513	
514	/// Resolves the example/project name from the CLI argument or context.
515	///
516	/// In workspace mode the name is required; in standalone mode it defaults
517	/// to the current directory name (which matches the package name from
518	/// `cargo oxide new`).
519	fn resolve_example_name(name: Option<String>, ctx: &commands::Context, subcommand: &str) -> String {
520	    if let Some(n) = name {
521	        return n;
522	    }
523	    if !ctx.is_workspace {
524	        return std::env::current_dir()
525	            .ok()
526	            .and_then(|p| p.file_name().map(|n| n.to_string_lossy().into_owned()))
527	            .unwrap_or_else(|| {
528	                eprintln!("Error: could not determine project name from current directory");
529	                std::process::exit(1);
530	            });
531	    }
532	    eprintln!("Error: <EXAMPLE> is required when running inside the cuda-oxide workspace.");
533	    eprintln!();
534	    eprintln!("Usage: cargo oxide {subcommand} <EXAMPLE>");
535	    eprintln!();
536	    eprintln!("Available examples are in crates/rustc-codegen-cuda/examples/");
537	    std::process::exit(1);
538	}
539	
540	/// Ensures an architecture is configured when `--emit-nvvm-ir` is used.
541	///
542	/// NVVM IR output is architecture-specific, so omitting every target source
543	/// would produce an unusable artifact. Exits with a descriptive error.
544	fn validate_nvvm_ir_arch(
545	    ctx: &commands::Context,
546	    example: &str,
547	    emit_nvvm_ir: bool,
548	    arch: Option<&str>,
549	) {
550	    if emit_nvvm_ir && !commands::has_configured_arch(ctx, arch) {
551	        eprintln!("Error: --emit-nvvm-ir requires a target architecture");
552	        eprintln!();
553	        eprintln!("NVVM IR output is architecture-specific. Pass --arch, set");
554	        eprintln!("CUDA_OXIDE_TARGET, or configure default-arch. For example:");
555	        eprintln!("  --arch sm_120    Blackwell (RTX 50 series)");
556	        eprintln!("  --arch sm_100    Blackwell");
557	        eprintln!();
558	        eprintln!("Example:");
559	        eprintln!("  cargo oxide run {} --emit-nvvm-ir --arch sm_120", example);
560	        std::process::exit(1);
561	    }
562	}

566	    use super::*;
567

content.content:
1	# Error Handling and Debugging
2	
3	GPU kernels fail differently from CPU code. The CUDA toolchain does not
4	support exceptions or stack unwinding today, there are no stack traces in
5	kernel output, and no `println!`. When something goes wrong, the result is
6	either silent data corruption, a hardware trap, or a cryptic driver error on the
7	host. This chapter covers cuda-oxide's tools for diagnosing and fixing kernel
8	problems.
9	
10	## What happens when a kernel goes wrong
11	
12	GPU errors fall into three categories:
13	
14	| Failure mode           | What you see                             | Example                                            |
15	|:-----------------------|:-----------------------------------------|:---------------------------------------------------|
16	| **Silent corruption**  | Wrong results, no error                  | Race condition, off-by-one index                   |
17	| **Hardware trap**      | `CUDA_ERROR_ILLEGAL_INSTRUCTION` on host | `gpu_assert!` failure, panic, OOB access           |
18	| **Launch failure**     | `DriverError` returned immediately       | Wrong grid dims, missing module, out of resources  |
19	
20	The CUDA toolchain does not expose an exception mechanism today (the hardware
21	could support it, but nvcc/ptxas do not wire it up). A trap instruction kills
22	the kernel and poisons the CUDA context -- subsequent operations on the same
23	context will fail until you handle or recreate it.
24	
25	## `gpu_printf!` -- printing from the GPU
26	
27	`gpu_printf!` lets you print values from device code for quick debugging. It
28	uses CUDA's built-in `vprintf` mechanism:
29	
30	```rust
31	use cuda_device::{kernel, thread, gpu_printf, DisjointSlice};
32	
33	#[kernel]
34	pub fn debug_kernel(data: &[f32], mut out: DisjointSlice<f32>) {
35	    let idx = thread::index_1d();
36	    if idx.get() < 4 {
37	        gpu_printf!("Thread {} sees value {}\n", idx.get(), data[idx.get()]);
38	    }
39	    if let Some(out_elem) = out.get_mut(idx) {
40	        *out_elem = data[idx.get()] * 2.0;
41	    }
42	}
43	```
44	
45	### Important details
46	
47	- **Flush requires sync.** Output is buffered on the GPU and only appears on
48	  the host after a stream or device synchronization (e.g., `to_host_vec` or
49	  `ctx.synchronize()`).
50	- **Buffer size.** The default printf buffer is 1 MiB. If many threads print,
51	  output may be truncated. Enlarge with
52	  `cudaDeviceSetLimit(cudaLimitPrintfFifoSize, size)`.
53	- **Thread ordering.** Output from different threads appears in arbitrary order.
54	- **Performance.** Printf serializes across threads -- avoid it in hot paths.
55	  Use it for debugging, not logging.
56	- **Format conversion.** The macro converts Rust `{}` format specifiers to C
57	  printf equivalents (`%d`, `%f`, etc.) at compile time.
58	
59	### Why not `println!` or `Debug`?
60	
61	Standard Rust formatting (`fmt::Display`, `fmt::Debug`, `format!`, `println!`)
62	requires dynamic dispatch, string allocation, and I/O -- none of which exist on
63	the GPU. `gpu_printf!` bypasses all of this by lowering directly to a CUDA
64	`vprintf` call.
65	
66	## `gpu_assert!` and `trap()`
67	
68	For fatal error checking on the device, use `gpu_assert!` or `debug::trap()`:
69	
70	```rust
71	use cuda_device::{kernel, thread, debug, gpu_assert, DisjointSlice};
72	
73	#[kernel]
74	pub fn checked_kernel(data: &[f32], len: u32, mut out: DisjointSlice<f32>) {
75	    let idx = thread::index_1d();
76	    gpu_assert!(idx.get() < len as usize);   // traps if false
77	
78	    if let Some(out_elem) = out.get_mut(idx) {
79	        *out_elem = data[idx.get()];
80	    }
81	}
82	```
83	
84	| Intrinsic                | What it does                | Host effect                                    |
85	|:-------------------------|:----------------------------|:-----------------------------------------------|
86	| `gpu_assert!(condition)` | Traps if condition is false | `CUDA_ERROR_ILLEGAL_INSTRUCTION`               |
87	| `debug::trap()`          | Unconditional trap          | `CUDA_ERROR_ILLEGAL_INSTRUCTION`               |
88	| `debug::breakpoint()`    | Emit `brkpt` instruction    | Pauses in cuda-gdb; crashes without debugger   |
89	
90	### The trap-and-check pattern
91	
92	A common workflow for catching device-side errors:
93	
94	```rust
95	// Launch kernel
96	// SAFETY: config matches vecadd's 1D indexing and all buffer bounds.
97	unsafe { module.vecadd(&stream, config, &a, &b, &mut c) }
98	    .expect("Launch failed");
99	
100	// Synchronize and check for traps
101	stream.synchronize().expect("Kernel trapped -- check gpu_assert! conditions");
102	```
103	
104	If a `gpu_assert!` fires, synchronization returns an error. The error message
105	doesn't tell you *which* assertion failed, so use `gpu_printf!` alongside
106	assertions to narrow down the problem.
107	
108	## Host-side error handling
109	
110	### `DriverError`
111	
112	The synchronous launch path returns
113	`Result<(), DriverError>`. The `DriverError` wraps a CUDA driver result code:
114	
115	```rust
116	// SAFETY: config matches vecadd's 1D indexing and all buffer bounds.
117	match unsafe { module.vecadd(&stream, config, &a, &b, &mut c) } {
118	    Ok(()) => { /* launched successfully */ }
119	    Err(e) => eprintln!("Launch failed: {e}"),
120	}
121	```
122	
123	### `DeviceError`
124	
125	The async path (`{kernel}_async` / `DeviceOperation`) uses `DeviceError`,
126	which wraps driver errors alongside context and scheduling failures:
127	
128	```rust
129	use cuda_async::error::DeviceError;
130	
131	let result: Result<Vec<f32>, DeviceError> = operation.sync();
132	```
133	
134	`DeviceError` variants include `Driver`, `Context`, `KernelCache`, `Scheduling`,
135	`Launch`, and `Internal`.
136	
137	### `CudaContext::check_err`
138	
139	After a series of operations, call `check_err()` on the context to surface any
140	asynchronous errors that may have been recorded:
141	
142	```rust
143	ctx.check_err().expect("Asynchronous GPU error detected");
144	```
145	
146	## `cargo oxide debug` -- cuda-gdb integration
147	
148	`cargo oxide debug` builds your kernel with debug info and launches cuda-gdb:
149	
150	```bash
151	cargo oxide debug vecadd          # Standard GDB
152	cargo oxide debug vecadd --tui    # GDB with TUI
153	cargo oxide debug vecadd --cgdb   # cgdb front-end
154	```
155	
156	By default this gives you **source-level debugging**: cuda-gdb can stop in
157	Rust source files and show a useful backtrace. Local-variable inspection is a
158	separate, heavier mode that you opt into when you need it.
159	
160	### Debug info modes
161	
162	cuda-oxide has three device debug modes:
163	
164	| Mode | How to enable it | What you get | Cost |
165	|:-----|:-----------------|:-------------|:-----|
166	| Off | default for normal `build` / `run` | Fastest generated PTX, no source mapping | none |
167	| Line tables | `cargo oxide debug`, or `CUDA_OXIDE_DEBUG=line-tables` | Source breakpoints, stepping, backtraces | low |
168	| Full | `CUDA_OXIDE_DEBUG=full cargo oxide debug <example>` | Line tables plus basic argument/local inspection | higher |
169	
170	Think of line tables as a map from machine instructions back to source lines:
171	
172	```text
173	PTX instruction  ──debug line table──>  src/main.rs:39
174	```
175	
176	Full debug adds variable records:
177	
178	```text
179	source local `tid`
180	      |
181	      v
182	LLVM/DWARF says: "tid lives in this stack slot"
183	      |
184	      v
185	cuda-gdb can try: print tid
186	```
187	
188	For local variables, the debugger also needs the current instruction to be
189	inside the same lexical scope as the variable:
190	
191	```text
192	function
193	  └─ if-let block
194	      └─ loop block
195	          └─ current instruction
196	```
197	
198	If you stop too early, such as at kernel launch or at the first helper call,
199	the variable may honestly print as `<optimized out>` because it has not been
200	loaded into a register yet. For variable checks, prefer a source line after the
201	value is used:
202	
203	```text
204	break src/main.rs:412
205	run
206	info args
207	info locals
208	```
209	
210	Seeing one variable as `<optimized out>` is not automatically a compiler bug;
211	it can mean "this value has no live machine location at this exact PC." Debug
212	info is a map, not a time machine.
213	
214	For inlined helper calls, cuda-oxide also keeps the original owner of each
215	argument. That matters because two different functions can both have an
216	argument numbered `1`:
217	
218	```text
219	kernel(data) calls helper(self)
220	
221	data -> arg #1 in kernel's debug scope
222	self -> arg #1 in helper's debug scope, with "inlined at" the kernel callsite
223	```
224	
225	Without that scope split, LLVM treats the metadata as contradictory and drops
226	it. Debug info is fussy like that; it wants the family tree, not just the
227	surname.
228	
229	Use line tables first. They are enough for most "where did execution go?"
230	questions, and they avoid the slower CUDA debug target mode. Use full debug
231	when you specifically want `print idx`, `print ptr`, or similar local-variable
232	inspection. Debuggers are allowed to be nosy; they are not always allowed to be
233	fast.
234	
235	The `CUDA_OXIDE_DEBUG` override works with `build`, `run`, `pipeline`, and
236	`debug`:
237	
238	```bash
239	CUDA_OXIDE_DEBUG=line-tables cargo oxide pipeline vecadd
240	CUDA_OXIDE_DEBUG=full cargo oxide debug vecadd
241	```
242	
243	Useful aliases:
244	
245	| Value | Meaning |
246	|:------|:--------|
247	| `off`, `none`, `0` | no device debug metadata |
248	| `line-tables`, `line`, `lines`, `1` | source line tables only |
249	| `full`, `2` | line tables plus basic variable metadata |
250	
251	### Why full debug turns optimization off
252	
253	Reliable local inspection and aggressive optimization pull in opposite
254	directions. An optimized value usually lives in a register only across the
255	short window where it is used; outside that window the debugger honestly has
256	nowhere to read it from, so `info locals` shows `<optimized out>`. The only way
257	to make a variable inspectable for its whole scope is to keep it in **memory**
258	and describe it with `llvm.dbg.declare`, the way every debug build does
259	(`gcc -O0`, `rustc` debug, and nvcc `-G`).
260	
261	So `CUDA_OXIDE_DEBUG=full` is a `-G`-style build. It automatically:
262	
263	- keeps every source local in its stack slot (skips Pliron `mem2reg`),
264	- skips annotated loop unrolling, which requires `mem2reg`'s SSA form,
265	- skips LLVM `opt -O2`, and
266	- runs `llc` at `-O0`,
267	
268	so the locals you see in cuda-gdb are real and stable. You do not need to set
269	`CUDA_OXIDE_NO_OPT=1` yourself; full mode implies it.
270	
271	| Setting | Meaning |
272	| :------ | :------ |
273	| `CUDA_OXIDE_DEBUG=off` | no device debug metadata; fully optimized PTX |
274	| `CUDA_OXIDE_DEBUG=line-tables` | source lines only; still optimized |
275	| `CUDA_OXIDE_DEBUG=full` | source lines plus locals/args; optimization off (`-G`) |
276	
277	Line tables stay on the optimized pipeline because a line map survives
278	optimization well; locals do not, which is why full mode steps off it.
279	
280	> The promotion-aware `mir.dbg_value` salvage that Pliron `mem2reg` performs is
281	> the building block for a future *optimized* debug tier (locals through
282	> `opt -O2`, best-effort). It is not what `full` uses today.
283	
284	### What works today
285	
286	Line-table mode supports:
287	
288	- breakpoints by kernel name, e.g. `break vecadd`
289	- source stepping and backtraces
290	- helper/inlined source locations from other files, such as stepping from your
291	  kernel into `cuda-device/src/thread.rs`
292	
293	Full mode (`-G`) supports inspecting:
294	
295	- local variables and arguments rustc exposes through `var_debug_info`
296	- scalar types (`bool`, integers, floats), raw pointers, and references
297	- structs, tuples, and fixed-size arrays, with their fields shown at the
298	  correct (real-layout) offsets, e.g.
299	  `out = DisjointSlice {ptr: 0x..., len: 1}` and `idx = ThreadIndex {raw: 0}`
300	
301	End-to-end behavior (breakpoint binds, backtrace, `info args`/`info locals`) is
302	checked on real hardware by `scripts/debug-smoketest.sh`.
303	
304	Full mode does **not** yet describe: enums (`Option`, `Result`, and other
305	multi-variant types), bare slice arguments split into a `(ptr, len)` pair at
306	the ABI boundary, closures, projections like `x.0`, or destructured variables.
307	Locals of an inlined helper frame may also show fewer entries than the kernel
308	frame; select the kernel frame (`frame 1`) to inspect kernel locals.
309	
310	### Breakpoint workflow
311	
312	1. Build with debug: `cargo oxide debug <example>`
313	2. Set a breakpoint on your kernel: `break vecadd`
314	3. Run: `run`
315	4. Inspect threads: `cuda thread`, `cuda block`, `cuda warp`
316	5. Print variables: `print idx`, `print *c_elem`
317	
318	`cargo oxide debug` selects the executable from Cargo's build artifact
319	metadata instead of assuming `target/release/<example>`. That keeps debugging
320	working with custom binary names, `package.default-run`, configured target
321	directories, host target triples, and virtual workspaces. Use `--bin <name>`
322	when a package has multiple binaries and no unambiguous default.
323	
324	For programmatic breakpoints, use `debug::breakpoint()` in your kernel code.
325	When cuda-gdb hits the `brkpt` instruction, it pauses execution and lets you
326	inspect the GPU state.
327	
328	:::{tip}
329	`debug::breakpoint()` will **crash** the kernel if no debugger is attached.
330	Guard it with a compile-time flag or only use it during debugging sessions.
331	:::
332	
333	## `cargo oxide sanitize` -- Compute Sanitizer
334	
335	For memory and synchronization correctness checks, run the same build path under
336	NVIDIA Compute Sanitizer:
337	
338	```bash
339	cargo oxide sanitize vecadd
340	cargo oxide sanitize sharedmem --tool racecheck
341	cargo oxide sanitize debug --tool synccheck -- --kernel-name kns=clock
342	cargo oxide sanitize my_app -- --leak-check full -- --app-flag value
343	```
344	
345	`memcheck` is the default tool. Use `racecheck` for shared-memory hazards,
346	`initcheck` for uninitialized global-memory reads, and `synccheck` for invalid
347	synchronization usage. Extra arguments after `--` are passed directly to
348	`compute-sanitizer` before the executable; use a second `--` to pass arguments
349	to the target program after the executable.
350	
351	The command enables optimized device line tables by default so findings can
352	name Rust source files and lines. An explicit `CUDA_OXIDE_DEBUG` process or
353	project setting still wins.
354	
355	Compute Sanitizer normally exits with status zero even when it reports a tool
356	finding. `cargo oxide sanitize` therefore supplies `--error-exitcode 86` by
357	default, making findings fail scripts and CI. An explicit sanitizer argument
358	overrides that default:
359	
360	```bash
361	# Intentionally keep a zero exit status and inspect the printed report.
362	cargo oxide sanitize vecadd -- --error-exitcode 0
363	```
364	
365	Options such as `--check-exit-code no` and `--require-cuda-init no` weaken what
366	a zero status proves. The wrapper therefore never declares the report clean
367	from process status alone; it reports completion and leaves the sanitizer
368	output visible for inspection.
369	
370	The `--no-fmad` CLI flag is forwarded through both ordinary and interop builds.
371	It keeps ordinary multiply and add/subtract operations separate, with one
372	rounding per operation. Explicit fused operations such as `f32::mul_add`
373	remain fused.
374	
375	NVVM IR and LTOIR builds also produce `.options` and versioned `.target`
376	sidecars. Keep both files with the artifact so later libNVVM and nvJitLink
377	steps preserve the same FMA policy.
378	
379	Run `memcheck` first when investigating memory safety. The other tools are
380	complementary and do not perform full memory-access checking.
381	
382	## `cargo oxide doctor` -- environment validation
383	
384	Before debugging kernel failures, verify your environment is correctly set up:
385	
386	```bash
387	cargo oxide doctor
388	```
389	
390	Doctor checks:
391	
392	| Check           | What it verifies                               |
393	|:----------------|:-----------------------------------------------|
394	| Rust toolchain  | Nightly compiler with required components      |
395	| Codegen backend | `librustc_codegen_cuda.so` built               |
396	| CUDA headers    | `cuda.h` present under the toolkit root        |
397	| CUDA toolkit    | `nvcc` found and version compatible            |
398	| libNVVM         | `libnvvm.so` loadable (libdevice math kernels) |
399	| nvJitLink       | `libnvJitLink.so` loadable (same)              |
400	| libdevice       | `libdevice.10.bc` discoverable (same)          |
401	| LLVM            | `llc` (21+) available for PTX generation       |
402	| Driver / GPU    | `nvidia-smi` reports a GPU and its compute cap |
403	
404	The libNVVM / nvJitLink / libdevice checks fire only when a kernel calls
405	CUDA libdevice math (`sin`, `cos`, `exp`, `pow`, `sqrt`, ...). If your
406	kernel is pure arithmetic, those three failing is harmless. They all ship
407	with the CUDA Toolkit -- no separate download. If any check fails, doctor
408	prints the standard install location for that component.
409	
410	Doctor itself needs neither the CUDA toolkit nor a driver, and it never
411	builds anything first, so it works on a machine where nothing is installed
412	yet. Two checks are informational rather than fatal: the codegen backend (a
413	missing `.so` just means "run `cargo oxide setup`"; `run`/`build` build it
414	on demand anyway) and the driver / GPU check (only `cargo oxide run` needs
415	a GPU; `build` and `pipeline` work without one).
416	
417	## `cargo oxide pipeline` -- inspecting the compilation
418	
419	When a kernel produces wrong results but no errors, inspect the compilation
420	pipeline to see exactly what code was generated:
421	
422	```bash
423	cargo oxide pipeline vecadd
424	```
425	
426	This prints the full pipeline output:
427	
428	1. **MIR collection** -- which functions the collector found
429	2. **`dialect-mir`** -- pliron IR modelling Rust MIR (before and after `mem2reg`)
430	3. **LLVM dialect** -- pliron IR modelling LLVM IR, provided by `pliron-llvm` (after `mir-lower`)
431	4. **Textual LLVM IR** -- serialized `.ll` file
432	5. **Final PTX** -- the generated assembly
433	
434	### Environment variables
435	
436	For more targeted inspection:
437	
438	| Variable                       | Effect                            |
439	|:-------------------------------|:----------------------------------|
440	| `CUDA_OXIDE_VERBOSE=1`         | Verbose compiler output           |
441	| `CUDA_OXIDE_SHOW_RUSTC_MIR=1`  | Dump the rustc MIR before import  |
442	| `CUDA_OXIDE_DEBUG=line-tables` | Emit source line-table metadata   |
443	| `CUDA_OXIDE_DEBUG=full`        | Emit full metadata for basic locals and args |
444	
445	## Profiling with Nsight Compute
446	
447	For performance debugging, NVIDIA's **Nsight Compute** (`ncu`) provides
448	roofline analysis, memory throughput, and occupancy metrics:
449	
450	```bash
451	ncu --set full ./target/release/my_example
452	```
453	
454	cuda-oxide kernels can emit profiler triggers using
455	`debug::prof_trigger::<N>()`, which generates a `pmevent` instruction that
456	Nsight Compute and Nsight Systems can capture for timeline annotation.
457	
458	:::{seealso}
459	[Nsight Compute Documentation](https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-compute/)
460	for the full profiling toolkit.
461	:::
462	
463	## Common pitfalls
464	
465	| Pitfall                          | Symptom                                    | Fix                                                              |
466	|:---------------------------------|:-------------------------------------------|:-----------------------------------------------------------------|
467	| Race condition on output buffer  | Wrong results, non-deterministic           | Use `DisjointSlice` plus matching prepared launch geometry      |
468	| Missing `sync_threads()`         | Stale shared memory reads                  | Add barrier between writes and reads                             |
469	| Wrong `shared_mem_bytes`         | `LAUNCH_OUT_OF_RESOURCES` or garbage data  | Match `LaunchConfig` to actual `DynamicSharedArray` usage        |
470	| Out-of-bounds with raw pointers  | Trap or silent corruption                  | Use `DisjointSlice::get_mut` for bounds checking                 |
471	| `panic!("message")` in kernel    | Compile error (fmt unavailable)            | Use `gpu_assert!` or `debug::trap()`                             |
472	| Forgetting to sync after launch  | Host reads stale data                      | Call `to_host_vec`, `stream.synchronize()`, or `.sync()`         |
473	| PTX built for wrong arch         | `NO_BINARY_FOR_GPU`                        | Rebuild with `cargo oxide build --arch sm_XX`                    |
474	
475	```{figure} images/debug-workflow.svg
476	:align: center
477	:width: 100%
478	
479	Debugging decision tree: kernel problems fall into three categories (compile
480	error, runtime trap, silent corruption), each with different diagnostic tools.
481	Common fixes are shown at the bottom.
482	```
483
